title: Signatures of unconventional superconductivity near reentrant and fractional quantum anomalous Hall insulators
abstract: Two-dimensional moiré Chern bands provide an exceptional platform for exploring a variety of many-body quantum phases at zero magnetic field within a lattice system. Flat Chern bands can, in principle, support exotic superconducting phases together with fractional topological phases. We report the observation of integer and fractional quantum anomalous Hall effects, the reentrant quantum anomalous Hall effect, and superconductivity within the first moiré Chern band of twisted bilayer MoTe2. This presents the first example of superconductivity emerging within a flat Chern band that simultaneously hosts fractional quantum anomalous effects, a phenomenon never observed in any other systems. The superconducting phase emerges from a normal state exhibiting anomalous Hall effects and sustains a large perpendicular critical magnetic field.
arxiv_affiliations: School of Physics and Astronomy, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

summary_segments.text: Transport measurements on twisted bilayer MoTe2 reveal integer and fractional quantum anomalous Hall effects, a reentrant quantum anomalous Hall effect, and superconductivity, all hosted within the first moiré Chern band at zero magnetic field.
The superconducting phase develops out of a normal state that itself displays anomalous Hall behavior, and it withstands a large perpendicular critical magnetic field.

This is reported as the first case in which superconductivity arises inside a flat Chern band that concurrently supports fractional quantum anomalous Hall states, a coexistence not seen in other platforms.

summary_segments.note: Situates the flat-Chern-band setting for a specialist reader
summary_segments.text: Twisted bilayer MoTe2 realizes topological moiré flat bands carrying nonzero Chern number, which support fractional quantum anomalous Hall states at zero field — the lattice analogue of fractional quantum Hall physics without an external magnetic field. Co-locating superconductivity in the same band is of interest for whether pairing can inherit nontrivial band topology.

summary_segments.note: Clarifies why the normal-state anomalous Hall response is notable
summary_segments.text: Superconductivity emerging from a time-reversal-symmetry-broken, anomalous-Hall normal state is a natural route to consider unconventional or topological pairing, since the parent state is not a conventional Fermi liquid.

significance_segments.note: Why the coexistence matters for the field
significance_segments.text: Observing superconductivity in the same flat Chern band that hosts fractional quantum anomalous Hall states places pairing and fractionalized topological order in direct proximity within one tunable system, sharpening the experimental question of whether superconductivity in such bands is unconventional and topologically nontrivial.

Because the superconductivity emerges from an anomalous-Hall normal state and survives a large perpendicular critical field, the result opens an experimental handle for probing the symmetry and robustness of pairing in topological moiré flat bands.

title: Robust Pretty Good Measurement via Hybrid Classical-Quantum Pseudoinverse Approximation and Circuit-Level Realization
abstract: Pretty Good Measurement (PGM) is a near-optimal strategy for quantum state discrimination, but its practical realization becomes unstable when the ensemble operator is singular or ill-conditioned. We introduce a numerically robust PGM formulation based on the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse, replacing the standard inverse square root with a threshold-regularized variant that remains well-defined across different spectral regimes. We develop a hybrid classical-quantum framework that combines pseudoinverse-based spectral preprocessing with quantum circuit realizations using block-encoding and spectral-transformation techniques. The framework incorporates support awareness, yielding physically meaningful measurement operators even in rank-deficient cases, and employs oblivious amplitude amplification to improve circuit-level success probabilities. Extensive numerical and circuit-level simulations show close agreement between theoretical predictions and quantum circuit outputs. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets, including ill-conditioned and degenerate scenarios, demonstrate stable discrimination performance where standard PGM becomes numerically unstable. The results establish a practical hybrid classical-quantum framework for robust quantum state discrimination and extend previous circuit-based implementations of the PGM testing stage toward pseudoinverse-aware measurement design.

summary_segments.text: The authors reformulate Pretty Good Measurement (PGM) for quantum state discrimination using a threshold-regularized Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse in place of the standard inverse square root, keeping the measurement well-defined when the ensemble operator is singular or ill-conditioned.

They develop a hybrid classical-quantum scheme combining pseudoinverse-based spectral preprocessing with circuit realizations via block-encoding and spectral-transformation techniques, using support awareness and oblivious amplitude amplification to handle rank-deficient cases and improve success probability.
Numerical and circuit-level simulations on synthetic and real datasets show stable discrimination in degenerate and ill-conditioned regimes where standard PGM becomes numerically unstable.

summary_segments.note: Places PGM in the state-discrimination landscape
summary_segments.text: Pretty Good Measurement is a canonical near-optimal POVM construction for distinguishing nonorthogonal quantum states; its standard form requires inverting the average ensemble density operator, which fails gracefully only when that operator has full rank. Regularizing via pseudoinverse is a natural route to robustness for near-term, noisy implementations.

significance_segments.note: Why a numerically robust formulation is useful
significance_segments.text: Making PGM well-behaved on singular and degenerate ensembles, together with an explicit block-encoding circuit recipe, addresses a practical obstacle to deploying state-discrimination primitives on near-term hardware, where ill-conditioning is common.

title: QuBE/Qubex: an integrated hardware-software system for superconducting qubit experiments with broadband control
abstract: Achieving high-fidelity operation in large-scale superconducting qubit systems requires not only control hardware with broad frequency coverage, low crosstalk, and tight synchronization but also software that coordinates system configuration, experiment execution, and data analysis. Here we present an integrated qubit-control system that combines broadband microwave hardware with a pulse-level software stack for scalable superconducting qubit experiments. The hardware provides broadband microwave coverage, including an instantaneous span of up to 1.6 GHz from a control output, while the software reduces setup and calibration overhead through automated configuration and built-in experiment workflows. We validate the system on a 64-qubit fixed-frequency transmon chip through full-chip frequency identification and representative demonstrations, including multi-unit far-detuned cross-resonance calibration and benchmarking that yields a measured two-qubit gate fidelity of 98.34%, and multilevel readout beyond the computational subspace. By disclosing the hardware architecture and releasing the software stack as open source, this work provides an inspectable hardware-software foundation for scalable superconducting qubit control experiments.

summary_segments.text: The authors present QuBE/Qubex, an integrated control system pairing broadband microwave hardware with a pulse-level software stack for scalable superconducting qubit experiments, with an instantaneous span up to 1.6 GHz from a single control output and automated configuration and calibration workflows.

The system is validated on a 64-qubit fixed-frequency transmon chip, including full-chip frequency identification, multi-unit far-detuned cross-resonance calibration, a measured two-qubit gate fidelity of 98.34%, and multilevel readout beyond the computational subspace.
The hardware architecture is disclosed and the software stack released as open source, providing an inspectable hardware-software foundation for superconducting qubit control.

summary_segments.note: Why broadband, synchronized control matters at scale
summary_segments.text: Scaling fixed-frequency transmon processors requires control electronics with wide frequency coverage, tight timing synchronization, and low crosstalk, together with software that manages calibration overhead that otherwise grows rapidly with qubit count.

significance_segments.note: Value of an open, integrated stack
significance_segments.text: An openly documented, co-designed hardware-software control system lowers the barrier for groups building mid-scale superconducting processors and makes calibration and benchmarking procedures reproducible, which is increasingly a bottleneck as device counts grow.

title: Quantum charge pumping in helical systems: A comparative study of short- and long-range hopping
abstract: Using the Keldysh non-equilibrium Green's function approach, we investigate charge pumping through a single-stranded helical structure described by a tight-binding model that includes either short-range hopping (SRH) or long-range hopping (LRH). While quantum pumping has been studied in various low-dimensional systems, the detailed behavior of the spectral current and the pumped dc current in helical geometries in the presence of higher-order electron hopping (beyond nearest neighbors) has not yet been systematically explored. Here, we focus on the interplay between helicity and extended hopping ranges, analyzing how they jointly control the energy-resolved and dc pumped currents under time-periodic end potentials. For LRH, the pumped dc current exhibits pronounced plateau-like regions as a function of chemical potential when energy levels are sparsely spaced -- consistent with adiabatic transport -- whereas SRH yields more parameter-sensitive currents without clear plateaus. The plateau stability is controlled by the drive frequency: at higher frequencies, Floquet side-band mixing destroys the plateaus, leading to oscillatory currents. The phase dependence remains nearly sinusoidal, and the current vanishes at zero phase lag, confirming the necessity of out-of-phase potentials. Crucially, in helical systems, the decay exponent $(\ell_c)$ acts as an effective structural parameter that can tune both the magnitude and sign of the pumped current, offering a geometric knob for controlling quantum pumping. Our findings not only fill a gap in the understanding of spectral and pumped currents in helical systems with extended hopping but also provide tools that can be applied to analyze similar phenomena in other chiral or quasi-one-dimensional systems.

summary_segments.text: Using the Keldysh nonequilibrium Green's function method, the authors study adiabatic-style charge pumping through a single-stranded helical tight-binding chain with either short-range or long-range hopping, focusing on how helicity and extended hopping jointly shape the energy-resolved and dc pumped currents under time-periodic end potentials.

Long-range hopping produces plateau-like dc current regions versus chemical potential consistent with adiabatic transport, whereas short-range hopping gives more parameter-sensitive currents without clear plateaus; higher drive frequency destroys plateaus through Floquet side-band mixing.

The phase dependence is nearly sinusoidal and the current vanishes at zero phase lag, while the hopping decay exponent acts as a structural knob that tunes both the magnitude and sign of the pumped current.

summary_segments.note: Why helical geometry plus long-range hopping is of interest
summary_segments.text: Single-stranded helical conductors (e.g., DNA-like chains) naturally feature longer-range hopping set by the helix geometry; treating the hopping decay exponent as a tunable parameter connects molecular geometry to quantum pumping in a controllable way.

significance_segments.text: By isolating the role of hopping range in pumped current plateaus and sign control, the work supplies design rules transferable to other chiral or quasi-one-dimensional systems where geometry sets the effective hopping reach.

title: Understanding quantum behaviors of an electron in a uniform magnetic field alternatively
abstract: Quantum mechanically, an electron moving in a uniform magnetic field forms Landau levels. A curious feature is that for states with a negative angular quantum number, the total probability current vanishes, which appears to contradict the classical picture of cyclotron motion. While a geometric interpretation based on classical orbits exists, alternative interpretations remain of interest. In this paper, we examine the probability current density and identify a critical radius that naturally partitions the plane into an inner clockwise-flow region and an outer counterclockwise-flow region. We show that the vanishing total current results from an exact cancellation between these two regions. Furthermore, by defining a partitioned kinetic angular momentum with respect to the critical radius, we reveal an intrinsic competitive structure: the electron simultaneously carries two opposing rotational components. The negative quantum number manifests in the strength of the inner counter-rotation, while the net kinetic angular momentum remains positive. This bidirectional flow picture also provides a dynamical interpretation of the infinite degeneracy of Landau levels.

summary_segments.text: The authors revisit Landau levels of an electron in a uniform magnetic field and address the apparent paradox that states with negative angular quantum number carry vanishing total probability current despite the classical cyclotron picture.

They show the probability current density defines a critical radius splitting the plane into an inner clockwise-flow region and an outer counterclockwise-flow region, with the vanishing total current arising from exact cancellation between the two; a partitioned kinetic angular momentum reveals two opposing rotational components, the negative quantum number setting the strength of the inner counter-rotation while the net kinetic angular momentum stays positive.
This bidirectional-flow picture also gives a dynamical interpretation of the infinite Landau-level degeneracy.

The interplay of canonical versus kinetic (gauge-invariant) angular momentum in Landau problems is a recurring subtlety; a real-space current decomposition offers an intuitive complement to the standard algebraic treatment.

significance_segments.text: The result is primarily conceptual, offering a transparent resolution of a long-standing apparent contradiction in the quantum mechanics of charged particles in magnetic fields that may aid teaching and intuition rather than predicting new phenomena.

title: Observation of orbital-angular-momentum-driven temperature modulation via the spin Peltier effect
abstract: Angular-momentum transport provides a pathway for controlling energy flow in solids beyond conventional charge-based mechanisms. While spin currents are known to mediate spin-caloritronic phenomena such as the spin Peltier effect (SPE), the role of orbital angular momentum in heat transport remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate orbital-angular-momentum-driven temperature modulation via the SPE in yttrium iron garnet/Pt/CuOx heterostructures. Using wedge-shaped CuOx layers combined with spatially resolved active thermal measurement techniques, we map the continuous thickness dependence and quantitatively disentangle the spin- and orbital-current-mediated contributions within a single device. The orbital-mediated component exhibits a pronounced maximum at an intermediate thickness, revealing a characteristic length scale for interfacial orbital-angular-momentum generation and propagation at the Cu/CuOx interface. These results provide direct experimental evidence that charge-current-driven orbital angular momentum can drive SPE-induced temperature modulation, establishing interfacial orbital processes as an additional channel for heat transport and providing a pathway toward spin-orbit caloritronics.
groups: Ken-ichi Uchida (National Institute for Materials Science)

summary_segments.text: The authors demonstrate orbital-angular-momentum-driven temperature modulation via the spin Peltier effect in yttrium iron garnet/Pt/CuOx heterostructures, using wedge-shaped CuOx layers and spatially resolved active thermal measurements to map the continuous thickness dependence within a single device.

They quantitatively separate spin- and orbital-current-mediated contributions, finding the orbital component peaks at an intermediate CuOx thickness, which reveals a characteristic length scale for interfacial orbital-angular-momentum generation and propagation at the Cu/CuOx interface.

The measurements give direct evidence that charge-current-driven orbital angular momentum can produce spin-Peltier-induced temperature modulation, establishing interfacial orbital processes as an additional heat-transport channel.

Orbital angular momentum can be generated even in light metals lacking strong spin-orbit coupling, making orbital currents an attractive complement to spin currents; extending caloritronic effects to the orbital channel parallels recent work on the orbital Hall and orbital torque effects.

significance_segments.text: Demonstrating an orbital contribution to thermal transport and extracting its interfacial length scale establishes a measurable handle for spin-orbit caloritronics and suggests orbital degrees of freedom as a route to heat management in materials without requiring heavy elements.

title: Nonmonotonic temperature dependence of the thermopower of atomic-size gold contacts
abstract: We report measurements of the thermopower of atomic-size gold contacts realized by the mechanically controllable break junction (MCBJ) technique over a temperature range from 18 K to 295 K. A thermometer included in the lithographic structure close to the constriction provides a direct measurement of the temperature increase generated by heating one side of the contact with a focused laser beam. While the conductance histograms confirm the quantum nature of the transport, we observe a nonmonotonic temperature dependence of the ensemble-averaged thermopower with a minimum of $-2\,μ$VK$^{-1}$ at about 150 K. The values for the thermopower obtained at the lowest and the high temperature are compatible with values reported in the literature, but the nonmonotonic behavior in between disagrees with the expected linear dependence for quantum coherent conductors described by the Landauer formula. We develop a theoretical model based on an energy dependent transmission function that qualitatively reproduces the nonmonotonic behavior, but fails quantitatively. We therefore interpret our data as a result of phonon contributions to the thermopower beyond the Landauer model and with opposite sign than the classical phonon drag known from bulk systems. Our findings show that, firstly, the thermopower gives important insight into the transport properties of atomic-size structures and second that the linear approximation of the Landauer model has to be used with caution when studying more complex transport properties even for atomic contacts from free-electron metals.

summary_segments.text: The authors measure the thermopower of atomic-size gold contacts formed by mechanically controllable break junctions from 18 K to 295 K, using an on-chip thermometer near the constriction to directly read the laser-induced temperature rise.

While conductance histograms confirm quantum transport, the ensemble-averaged thermopower is nonmonotonic in temperature, reaching a minimum of about -2 microV/K near 150 K, contradicting the linear dependence expected for coherent conductors in the Landauer picture.

A model with an energy-dependent transmission function reproduces the nonmonotonic shape only qualitatively, leading the authors to attribute the behavior to phonon contributions beyond the Landauer model with opposite sign to classical bulk phonon drag.

Thermopower is sensitive to the energy derivative of the transmission at the Fermi level, making it a finer probe of the transmission spectrum than conductance alone, even for nominally simple free-electron metals like gold.

significance_segments.text: The finding that the linear Landauer approximation breaks down for thermopower even in atomic gold contacts signals that phonon effects must be included when interpreting thermoelectric transport in atomic-scale junctions.

title: Hall conductivity reveals the nature of quantum coherence in strongly correlated metals
abstract: Linear-in-temperature resistivity is a hallmark for strange metallic transport, and appears universally in many strongly correlated electron systems. However, the focus on the longitudinal channel often overshadows the profound microscopic insights contained within the transverse response. Here, we utilize numerically exact determinantal quantum Monte Carlo simulations of the doped Hubbard model in a magnetic field to calculate longitudinal and transverse transport. We demonstrate that while the resistivity is robustly $T$-linear across parameter sets, the Hall response is highly sensitive to particle-hole asymmetry, Fermi surface topology, and many-body correlation effects. Specifically, the combination of these effects determine a crossover scale in which the system becomes quantum-coherent, and is reflected in the Hall conductivity. Our results demonstrate that while the $T$-linearity in resistivity appears universal, the Hall response reveals a crossover from semi-classical to quantum-coherent transport otherwise masked in the longitudinal channel.

summary_segments.text: Using numerically exact determinantal quantum Monte Carlo on the doped Hubbard model in a magnetic field, the authors compute both longitudinal and transverse transport to test what the Hall response reveals beyond the much-studied longitudinal channel.

They find the resistivity is robustly T-linear across parameter sets, while the Hall response is highly sensitive to particle-hole asymmetry, Fermi-surface topology, and many-body correlations, and these together set a crossover scale at which the system becomes quantum-coherent.
The Hall conductivity therefore exposes a crossover from semiclassical to quantum-coherent transport that is masked in the apparently universal T-linear resistivity.

T-linear resistivity is a defining but ambiguous hallmark of strange metals, appearing across cuprates, heavy fermions, and moire systems; identifying a transverse diagnostic that distinguishes coherent from incoherent transport addresses a central open question about the nature of these states.

significance_segments.text: Establishing the Hall conductivity as a numerically grounded fingerprint of quantum coherence gives experimentalists and theorists a sharper tool to separate strange-metal transport from conventional Fermi-liquid behavior in strongly correlated systems.

title: Role of quantum confinement in semiconductor-superconductor core-shell nanowires
abstract: This work is motivated by the experimentally observed coherence of the supercurrent in semiconductor nanowires covered by a half-shell metallic superconductor, which leads to flux dependent supercurrent oscillations with period h/2e, as expected for a tubular superconductor, i.e. Little-Parks oscillations. We perform microscopic model calculations and compare the results for full and half metallic shells. We use an effective Hamiltonian derived from the Green's function of the proximitized semiconductor nanowire, where the presence of the superconductor is represented by a self energy. Furthermore, we incorporate the electrostatic band-bending at the metal-semiconductor interface as a rectangular narrow quantum well on the semiconductor side. The properties of the eigenstates of the effective Hamiltonian are determined by the spatial profile of the corresponding transverse modes in the normal state. For half-shell wires, transverse modes with high-enough energy expand outside the interface quantum well and generate eigenstates with mixed electron-hole character that surround the entire circumference of the nanowire, similar to eigenstates of the full-shell system. We identify these states as being responsible for the observed Little-Parks effect.

summary_segments.text: Motivated by observed h/2e Little-Parks supercurrent oscillations in semiconductor nanowires covered by a half-shell superconductor, the authors perform microscopic model calculations comparing full and half metallic shells on a hexagonal semiconductor core.

They use an effective Hamiltonian from the proximitized-nanowire Green's function, representing the superconductor by a self-energy and modeling interface band-bending as a narrow rectangular quantum well on the semiconductor side.

For half-shell wires, sufficiently high-energy transverse modes extend beyond the interface quantum well to form mixed electron-hole eigenstates encircling the full nanowire circumference, similar to the full-shell case, and the authors identify these states as responsible for the observed Little-Parks effect.

Full-shell and half-shell hybrid nanowires are platforms for topological superconductivity and Majorana proposals; understanding how quantum confinement in the core controls flux-periodic supercurrent coherence is important for interpreting transport in these devices.

significance_segments.text: Explaining how h/2e flux periodicity survives in half-shell geometries clarifies which states carry phase-coherent supercurrent around the core, informing the design and interpretation of hybrid nanowire devices that rely on tubular superconducting coherence.

title: Emergent mirror symmetry in the optimization of the central-spin quantum battery
abstract: Quantum batteries provide a useful setting for exploring nonequilibrium many-body effects in energy storage. Here we investigate the optimization of a quantum battery based on the central-spin model. We identify two complementary structural indicators associated with the effective charging dynamics: one yields an upper bound on the average charging power, while the other characterizes the buildup of stored energy. We show that these two indicators are jointly optimized at a distinguished initial charger excitation number, which selects a particular Dicke sector of the model. At this common optimal point, the effective charging Hamiltonian becomes exactly mirror symmetric, suggesting mirror symmetry as a useful structural indicator for optimizing quantum batteries. We further show that the corresponding optimal dynamics can be closely approximated by product initial states, in particular by spin coherent states whose excitation-number distribution is centered at the symmetry-selected point. Our results establish a direct connection between charging performance, optimal-state structure, and emergent symmetry in the central-spin quantum battery, and suggest symmetry as a useful organizing principle for efficient charging in interacting many-body quantum systems.

summary_segments.text: The authors optimize a quantum battery based on the central-spin model, introducing two structural indicators of the effective charging dynamics: one bounding the average charging power and one tracking the buildup of stored energy.

Both indicators are jointly optimized at a distinguished initial charger excitation number that selects a particular Dicke sector, where the effective charging Hamiltonian becomes exactly mirror symmetric, identifying mirror symmetry as a structural marker of optimal charging.
The optimal dynamics can be closely approximated by product initial states, in particular spin coherent states whose excitation-number distribution is centered at the symmetry-selected point.

Central-spin (Dicke-type) models are standard testbeds for collective charging advantages in quantum batteries; finding that an emergent symmetry not present in the bare Hamiltonian organizes the optimal protocol connects charging performance to many-body structure.

significance_segments.text: Identifying emergent mirror symmetry as an organizing principle for efficient charging suggests symmetry-based heuristics for designing optimal charging protocols in interacting many-body quantum batteries, rather than relying solely on numerical optimization.

title: Intrinsic Nonreciprocity in Electron-Phonon Interaction Driven Thermoelectric Diodes
abstract: We study an electron-phonon interaction driven thermoelectric diode. The nonreciprocity in this diode arises from the asymmetry between the probabilities of phonon emission and absorption in the electron-phonon interaction, as well as the structural reflection asymmetry. We reveal the intrinsic nature of this nonreciprocity, as the forward and backward electron transport remains asymmetric even when the applied temperature difference is not reversed. This intrinsic nonreciprocity gives rise to two novel transport phenomena. One is a novel thermoelectric effect which is driven by the temperature difference between the leads and the central device region, rather than the conventional temperature difference between the two leads. The second, and more significant, phenomenon is the suppression of electronic backscattering in the load resistor. This suppression decreases the resistance of the load resistor, which leads to the breakdown of Ohm's addition law. Under suitable conditions, the presence of electron-phonon interaction can yield a larger thermoelectric current compared to the case without it. This intrinsic nonreciprocity opens up a new pathway for low-power electronics besides topology and superconductivity, and for nonreciprocal thermoelectric devices.

summary_segments.text: The authors study a thermoelectric diode driven by electron-phonon interaction, where nonreciprocity originates from the asymmetry between phonon emission and absorption probabilities together with structural reflection asymmetry.

They argue this nonreciprocity is intrinsic, since forward and backward electron transport stay asymmetric even without reversing the applied temperature difference, and it produces a thermoelectric effect driven by the temperature difference between leads and the central region rather than between the two leads.

A second, more significant consequence is suppression of electronic backscattering in the load resistor, lowering its resistance and breaking Ohm's addition law, so that electron-phonon interaction can yield a larger thermoelectric current than its absence under suitable conditions.

Nonreciprocal electronic devices are usually engineered through broken time-reversal symmetry (magnetic fields), topology, or superconductivity; obtaining intrinsic nonreciprocity from inelastic electron-phonon scattering offers a distinct mechanism in mesoscopic structures.

significance_segments.text: Proposing electron-phonon interaction as a route to intrinsic nonreciprocity points toward low-power nonreciprocal and thermoelectric devices that do not require magnetic fields or superconductivity, broadening the design space for diode-like mesoscopic elements.

title: Enhanced localization length in a disordered one-dimensional band via cavity coupling to delocalized states
abstract: We investigate the localization properties of cavity-coupled electronic states in disordered systems, motivated by recent proposals of cavity-mediated hopping in quantum Hall systems. We first introduce a minimal two-band model in which localized states in a disordered one-dimensional band are coupled, through a homogeneous cavity mode, to an excited band of delocalized states. Combining perturbation theory with a transfer-matrix approach, we show that cavity-assisted hopping between localized states decays exponentially with distance, implying that the eigenstates remain localized even beyond the perturbative regime. Nevertheless, the corresponding localization length increases with the light-matter coupling strength and can extend over several lattice sites in the single-electron ultrastrong-coupling regime. We then study a disordered Landau band coupled to a cavity mode within the framework developed in Refs.[1,2]. We find that the effective cavity-mediated coupling between edge states also decays exponentially with distance, but with a localization length that can reach micrometer scales for experimentally realistic parameters. By analyzing the inverse participation ratio, we show that this enhanced coupling is predominantly mediated by the most extended states of the upper Landau band. Our results demonstrate that, while cavity-induced hopping in disordered quantum Hall systems remains exponentially localized, the associated localization length can become sufficiently large for the corresponding states to exhibit effectively delocalized behavior on mesoscopic length scales.

summary_segments.text: Motivated by proposals of cavity-mediated hopping in quantum Hall systems, the authors study a minimal two-band model in which disorder-localized states in a one-dimensional band couple, through a homogeneous cavity mode, to an excited band of delocalized states.

Combining perturbation theory with transfer-matrix methods, they show cavity-assisted hopping between localized states decays exponentially with distance, so eigenstates remain localized, yet the localization length grows with light-matter coupling and can span several lattice sites in the single-electron ultrastrong-coupling regime.

Applying the framework to a disordered Landau band coupled to a cavity, they find the effective edge-state coupling again decays exponentially but with a localization length reaching micrometer scales for realistic parameters, mediated predominantly by the most extended states of the upper Landau band.

Engineering electronic transport with vacuum cavity fields is an active direction following reports of cavity-modified transport in quantum Hall systems; clarifying whether cavity coupling can genuinely delocalize disorder-localized states, versus merely stretching the localization length, is central to interpreting such claims.

significance_segments.text: By showing cavity coupling enhances localization length without true delocalization, the work places a quantitative bound on cavity-mediated transport in disordered quantum Hall systems and identifies the mesoscopic regime where the effect can nonetheless mimic delocalized behavior.

title: Diffusive Relaxation of Participation Entropy in U(1)-symmetric Dynamics
abstract: Participation entropy (PE) quantifies the spread of a many-body wavefunction across configuration space. While PE relaxes rapidly in generic chaotic systems, we show that $\mathrm{U}(1)$ conservation laws slow it down by imprinting with the slow hydrodynamic modes. Using a cluster expansion around equilibrium, we show that, after local density inhomogeneities decay, the leading PE deficit is dominated by squared connected density correlations. The long time relaxation is therefore controlled by diffusive correlation spreading, giving $ΔS(t)\sim t^{-1/2}$ in the hydrodynamic regime and crossing over to $\sim \exp[-O(t/L^2)]$ when $t\geq L^2$. We confirm this entropy correlation relation using exact computation and infinite system tensor network simulations in various quantum $\mathrm{U}(1)$ conserving circuits. Our results establish PE as a sensitive probe of hydrodynamic memory and suggest that slow relaxation is a generic consequence of conservation laws.

summary_segments.text: The authors study participation entropy (PE), which quantifies how a many-body wavefunction spreads across configuration space, and show that U(1) conservation laws slow its relaxation by imprinting it with the slow hydrodynamic modes, unlike the rapid relaxation in generic chaotic systems.

Using a cluster expansion around equilibrium, they find that once local density inhomogeneities decay, the leading PE deficit is governed by squared connected density correlations, giving a diffusive long-time relaxation of the entropy deficit scaling as t^{-1/2} in the hydrodynamic regime and crossing over to exp[-O(t/L^2)] once t exceeds L^2.
The entropy-correlation relation is confirmed with exact computation and infinite-system tensor-network simulations across various U(1)-conserving circuits and Hamiltonian dynamics.

Connecting an information-theoretic diagnostic to emergent hydrodynamics extends the program relating entanglement and operator-spreading dynamics to conserved charges, where U(1) symmetry is known to produce diffusive tails in quantities such as the second Renyi entropy and out-of-time-order correlators.

significance_segments.text: Establishing participation entropy as a sensitive probe of hydrodynamic memory, and showing slow relaxation is a generic consequence of conservation laws, gives a measurable bridge between wavefunction-structure diagnostics and the transport coefficients of conserved quantities in many-body dynamics.

title: A Pfaffian quantum Hall state of ultracold bosons
abstract: Fractional quantum Hall states are a cornerstone of topological physics, hosting fractionally charged quasiparticles with exotic statistics that promise to enable topologically protected quantum information processing. Among these, the Pfaffian state introduced by Moore and Read implements a p-wave pairing structure that supports excitations with non-Abelian exchange statistics. Despite extensive study in electronic systems, direct access to its pairing structure has remained limited. Here we realize a three-particle bosonic Pfaffian state of ultracold $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$ atoms in an optical lattice subject to a Floquet-engineered synthetic magnetic field. Using a Bayesian-optimized adiabatic protocol, we prepare a state exhibiting Pfaffian pairing correlations. Site-resolved measurements of multi-point density correlations reveal a pronounced suppression of short-range three-body coincidences, reflecting the underlying pairing structure. We further probe the state's transport response through Hall drift measurements. Our results establish a bottom-up approach to engineering non-Abelian topological order and lay the groundwork for future explorations of anyonic braiding in synthetic matter.

summary_segments.text: The authors realize a three-particle bosonic analog of the Moore-Read Pfaffian state using ultracold 87Rb atoms in an optical lattice subject to a Floquet-engineered synthetic magnetic field, prepared via a Bayesian-optimized adiabatic protocol.

Site-resolved measurements of multi-point density correlations reveal a pronounced suppression of short-range three-body coincidences, reflecting the underlying p-wave pairing structure, and the state's transport response is probed through Hall drift measurements.
The work establishes a bottom-up route to engineering non-Abelian topological order and groundwork for exploring anyonic braiding in synthetic matter.

summary_segments.note: Why the Pfaffian state is sought
summary_segments.text: The Moore-Read Pfaffian state hosts non-Abelian excitations central to topological quantum computation; in electronic systems it is associated with the nu=5/2 fractional quantum Hall plateau, where direct access to its pairing structure has been difficult, motivating clean, microscope-resolved realizations in synthetic platforms.

significance_segments.text: Assembling a Pfaffian state atom-by-atom with single-site readout gives direct microscopic access to its pairing correlations, something electronic experiments cannot easily provide, and offers a controllable platform for testing non-Abelian topological order and, prospectively, anyonic braiding.

title: Substrate insulated Josephson junctions for superconducting quantum circuits
abstract: We have developed a fabrication technique for Josephson junctions that employs a three-dimensional patterned, low-loss substrate instead of commonly used organic resists. The technique enables the fabrication of high-quality trilayer junctions from a wide range of geometries and materials, including high-melting-point superconductors such as tantalum or niobium. The junction electrodes are free from intentionally introduced oxides and organic materials, which are known sources of decoherence. We fabricate and characterize underdamped Nb/AlOx/Nb junctions of different sizes in several geometries. Such junctions enable manufacturing of quantum circuits operating at higher speeds and elevated temperatures.
arxiv_affiliations: DVGW-Forschungsstelle am Engler-Bunte-Institut des Karlsruher Instituts für Technologie

summary_segments.text: The authors develop a Josephson junction fabrication technique that uses a three-dimensional patterned, low-loss substrate in place of the organic resists commonly used for shadow-evaporated junctions.

The method enables high-quality trilayer junctions across a wide range of geometries and materials, including high-melting-point superconductors such as tantalum and niobium, with electrodes free of intentionally introduced oxides and organic materials that are known decoherence sources.
They fabricate and characterize underdamped Nb/AlOx/Nb junctions of various sizes and geometries, arguing such junctions enable quantum circuits operating at higher speeds and elevated temperatures.

summary_segments.note: Why junction materials and cleanliness matter
summary_segments.text: Aluminum-based junctions dominate transmon qubits but cap operating temperature and gap energy; resist residues at junction interfaces are a recognized source of two-level-system loss, so resist-free trilayer junctions in higher-gap materials are attractive for higher-temperature or higher-speed superconducting circuits.

significance_segments.text: A resist-free fabrication route compatible with refractory, high-gap superconductors broadens the materials available for low-loss Josephson elements and points toward superconducting circuits that operate at higher frequencies and temperatures than aluminum permits.

title: Statistical Mechanics and Symmetries of Non-Abelian Anyon Proliferation: From Deformation to Decoherence
abstract: Topological quantum computation relies on braiding non-Abelian anyons, but requires the underlying topological order to survive imperfect state preparation and environmental noise. We show that the instability of topological order to wavefunction deformations and to decoherence, with the latter probed by syndrome distributions, are generically captured by stat-mech models whose symmetries naturally expose the corrupting anyonic excitations. As an example, we combine this framework with Monte-Carlo simulations to resolve the stability of $D_4$ topological order under deformations and quantum channels that proliferate multiple non-Abelian anyon species that individually are unable to condense. We show that beyond a finite threshold, proliferation of two non-Abelian anyon species parasitically condenses a shared Abelian-anyon fusion outcome, destroying the topological order. Our symmetry-based approach sharply differentiates the resulting trivial phase from that obtained by condensing all Abelian charges; in other words, the trivial phase "remembers" which anyons condensed. This framework provides a first step into identifying the relevant symmetry for optimal decoders, conditioned on syndrome measurements, of non-Abelian topological order.

summary_segments.text: The authors show that the instability of topological order to wavefunction deformations and to decoherence (the latter probed via syndrome distributions) is generically captured by statistical-mechanics models whose symmetries expose the corrupting anyonic excitations.

Combining this framework with Monte-Carlo simulations, they resolve the stability of D4 topological order under deformations and quantum channels that proliferate multiple non-Abelian anyon species which individually cannot condense, finding that beyond a finite threshold two such species parasitically condense a shared Abelian fusion outcome and destroy the order.

Their symmetry-based approach distinguishes the resulting trivial phase from one obtained by condensing all Abelian charges, so the trivial phase retains a memory of which anyons condensed, providing a first step toward identifying symmetries for optimal syndrome-conditioned decoders of non-Abelian topological order.

Mapping mixed-state topological order under decoherence to classical stat-mech models has become a standard route to locate error thresholds; extending it to non-Abelian orders, where condensation can proceed through shared fusion channels, addresses a genuinely harder and less-understood regime relevant to braiding-based quantum computation.

significance_segments.text: Providing a unified, symmetry-resolved description of how non-Abelian topological order degrades under deformation and noise sharpens the theory of error thresholds in these phases and lays groundwork for decoders tailored to non-Abelian codes.

title: Robust Spin Logic Enabled by Generalized $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ Symmetry in $p$-Wave Magnets
abstract: Unconventional magnets combine the vanishing stray fields of antiferromagnets with the strong spin-splitting of ferromagnets, offering a unique material platform for spintronics. However, a critical challenge in realizing functional spin-logic devices lies in preserving long-range spin coherence against momentum-degrading scattering and gate-induced dephasing. Here, we demonstrate that the intrinsic momentum-dependent exchange field of a three-dimensional $p$-wave magnet can be precisely tuned against gate-induced Rashba spin-orbit coupling to establish a \textit{generalized} $\mathrm{SU}(2)$ spin-rotation symmetry. This emergent conservation law generates a symmetry-protected Persistent Spin Helix (PSH), effectively integrating the high energy scales of 3D bulk magnetic exchange with the macroscopic coherence of symmetry protection. By modeling a synergistic $p$-wave magnetic spin field-effect transistor (spin-FET), we reveal high-visibility Datta-Das conductance oscillations controlled purely by electrical gating. Crucially, our quantum transport simulations confirm that this symmetry-engineered transport regime exhibits exceptional resilience against strong non-magnetic Anderson disorder and geometric variations. These results establish a synergistic paradigm for non-magnetized spintronics, demonstrating how the active integration of spin-orbit coupling and unconventional magnetism can yield disorder-resilient spintronic logic.

summary_segments.text: The authors show that the intrinsic momentum-dependent exchange field of a three-dimensional p-wave magnet can be tuned against gate-induced Rashba spin-orbit coupling to establish a generalized SU(2) spin-rotation symmetry, which generates a symmetry-protected persistent spin helix combining bulk magnetic exchange energy scales with the macroscopic coherence of symmetry protection.
Modeling a p-wave magnetic spin field-effect transistor, they obtain high-visibility Datta-Das conductance oscillations controlled purely by electrical gating.
Quantum transport simulations confirm that this symmetry-engineered regime is exceptionally resilient against strong non-magnetic Anderson disorder and geometric variations.

The persistent spin helix arises when Rashba and Dresselhaus-type couplings are balanced to restore an SU(2) symmetry that protects spin coherence against momentum scattering; realizing this protection using the exchange field of p-wave magnets ties it to the recent surge of interest in altermagnets and other unconventional magnets that combine vanishing stray fields with strong spin splitting.

significance_segments.text: Demonstrating disorder-resilient, electrically controlled spin transport from a symmetry engineered out of unconventional magnetism plus spin-orbit coupling offers a concrete design paradigm for non-magnetized spintronic logic that does not rely on stray fields or fine material tuning.

title: Cyclotron mass-selective de Haas-van Alphen measurements using temperature modulation
abstract: We present a temperature-modulated de Haas-van Alphen measurement technique that allows selective addressing of quantum oscillations with different effective masses $m^{\ast}$ using a non-monotonic amplitude evolution with temperature and magnetic field, governed by the temperature derivative of the Lifshitz-Kosevich factor. The technique relies on harmonic modulation of the sample temperature and phase-sensitive detection of quantum oscillations in the voltage induced in a pick-up coil. We use a set of frequencies with strong Zeeman-driven harmonic content in the compensated topological semimetal MoSi$_{2}$ as a natural linear mass comb ranging from 1$m^{\ast}$ to 13$m^{\ast}$ to demonstrate the tunability of the mass-dependent quantum oscillation amplitudes experimentally. The technique allows to reliably isolate weak contributions of heavy orbits that are inaccessible in conventional de Haas-van Alphen frequency spectra because their frequency peaks overlap with much stronger frequency peaks of lighter orbits.
groups: Marc A. Wilde (Technical University of Munich)

summary_segments.text: The authors present a temperature-modulated de Haas-van Alphen technique that selectively addresses quantum oscillations of different effective masses, exploiting the non-monotonic amplitude evolution with temperature and field governed by the temperature derivative of the Lifshitz-Kosevich factor, via harmonic temperature modulation and phase-sensitive detection of pick-up-coil voltage.

Using Zeeman-driven harmonics in the compensated topological semimetal MoSi2 as a linear mass comb spanning 1 to 13 times the base effective mass, they demonstrate experimental tunability of the mass-dependent oscillation amplitudes.

The method reliably isolates weak contributions from heavy orbits that are inaccessible in conventional dHvA spectra because their frequency peaks overlap with much stronger peaks from lighter orbits.

Quantum-oscillation spectroscopy is a primary tool for mapping Fermi surfaces, but heavy-mass orbits are strongly thermally damped and easily buried under dominant light-mass frequencies; a mass-selective filter directly addresses this long-standing limitation of the Lifshitz-Kosevich analysis.

significance_segments.text: Turning the effective mass into a selection knob extends quantum-oscillation spectroscopy to weak heavy-orbit contributions otherwise masked in the frequency spectrum, improving Fermi-surface characterization in multiband metals and topological semimetals.

title: Hybrid Hamiltonian-diagrammatic quantum impurity solver
abstract: Quantum impurity models, which describe the coupling between interacting orbitals and a non-interacting bath, play a central role in the physics of strongly correlated electron systems. Solving a quantum impurity model in general requires the use of non-perturbative numerical methods. Hamiltonian-based approaches, which rely on an explicit bath discretization, are typically limited to a small number of bath sites or small entanglement, and diagrammatic methods suffer from sign problems, slow convergence, or diagram truncation approximations. Here we show that these two classes of methods can be combined: augmenting diagrammatic methods with a small auxiliary bath can reduce the residual problem to a regime where low-order perturbation theory is highly accurate and rapidly converging. In a simple benchmark, the precision of the hybrid approach surpasses bold-line calculations by several orders of magnitude; for a strongly interacting two-orbital model with a severe sign problem, convergence is achieved at three orders of magnitude lower computational cost than competing methods; and convergence to the unknown exact result is rapidly accelerated in a difficult realistic problem. Our results establish a practical route to high-precision quantum impurity solutions in correlated quantum systems.

summary_segments.text: The authors present a quantum impurity solver that combines Hamiltonian-based and diagrammatic approaches: augmenting diagrammatic methods with a small auxiliary bath reduces the residual problem to a regime where low-order perturbation theory is highly accurate and rapidly convergent, sidestepping the bath-size limits of Hamiltonian methods and the sign and convergence problems of purely diagrammatic ones.

In a simple benchmark the hybrid precision surpasses bold-line calculations by several orders of magnitude; for a strongly interacting two-orbital model with a severe sign problem, convergence is reached at three orders of magnitude lower cost than competing methods; and convergence is markedly accelerated in a difficult realistic problem.

Quantum impurity solvers are the computational engine of dynamical mean-field theory and its extensions for strongly correlated materials; the perennial tension between exponential Hilbert-space growth in Hamiltonian methods and sign problems in continuous-time Monte Carlo makes a hybrid that mitigates both broadly useful.

significance_segments.text: Establishing a practical route to high-precision impurity solutions at substantially reduced cost, including in sign-problematic multi-orbital settings, can improve the accuracy and reach of DMFT-based studies of correlated electron systems.

title: Spin polarisation signatures of Fractionally Charged Skyrmions in Fractional Quantum Hall states
abstract: We investigate spin polarisation and low-energy excitations in fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states using cavity-polariton spectroscopy of high-mobility GaAs quantum wells. By measuring the optical coupling strength of interband Landau-level excitations over the range $1/3 \le ν\le 1$, we extract the spin polarisation of the electron system as a function of filling factor. Complete suppression of the oscillator strength of the lowest energy excitation, characteristic of singlet trion formation in fully polarised systems, is reported for the first time in this regime. At large magnetic fields, fully polarised FQH states exhibit symmetric depolarisation away from their quantised fillings, analogous to Skyrmionic behaviour near $ν=1$. The depolarisation follows an empirical law $S=ν^*$, where $S$ is the number of spin flips per added magnetic flux quantum and $ν^*$ the effective Composite Fermion filling factor. We interpret this behaviour as evidence for Minimal Fractionally Charged Skyrmions (MFCS) formed from bound spin-flip and quasiparticle excitations.

summary_segments.text: The authors probe spin polarization and low-energy excitations of fractional quantum Hall states in high-mobility GaAs quantum wells using cavity-polariton spectroscopy, extracting the electron spin polarization versus filling factor from the optical coupling strength of interband Landau-level excitations over the range 1/3 <= nu <= 1.

They report, for the first time in this regime, complete suppression of the lowest-energy excitation's oscillator strength characteristic of singlet trion formation in fully polarized systems, and find that fully polarized FQH states depolarize symmetrically away from their quantized fillings, analogous to skyrmion behavior near nu=1.

The depolarization follows an empirical law S = nu*, with S the number of spin flips per added flux quantum and nu* the effective composite-fermion filling, which they interpret as evidence for minimal fractionally charged skyrmions formed from bound spin-flip and quasiparticle excitations.

summary_segments.note: Skyrmions in the quantum Hall regime
summary_segments.text: Near nu=1, the lowest charged excitations are spin-textured skyrmions rather than single spin flips; the fractional quantum Hall analogue, where composite-fermion physics predicts fractionally charged skyrmions, has been harder to access optically, making spin-polarization spectroscopy a valuable probe.

significance_segments.text: Optical evidence for fractionally charged skyrmions across the FQH regime supplies a spectroscopic handle on the spin structure of composite-fermion excitations, complementing transport measurements and testing predictions for spin-textured quasiparticles in fractional states.

title: Fractional shot noise of an SU(N) Kondo system
abstract: We consider transport through a multi-level interacting quantum dot (N-QD) in Kondo regime. Using the Kotliar-Ruckentein slave boson approach (SBMFA) for N-level Anderson model we define effectively noninteracting quasiparticles of the SU(N) Kondo system ($N=2,3,4,5,6$). Kondo resonance transmission coefficients determine linear noise describing quasiparticle partitioning. To discuss nonlinear conductance, susceptibilities and shot noise in the strong coupling regime, we apply Fermi liquid theory with parameters expressed by susceptibilities of pseudofermions determined within SBMFA. Nonlinear shot noise is dominated by two-quasiparticle scattering. However, we demonstrate that for occupation regions distant from the electron-hole symmetry point, the role of three-body correlations must be revealed.
groups: Stanisław Lipiński (Institute of Molecular Physics, Polish Academy of Sciences)

summary_segments.text: The authors study transport through a multi-level interacting quantum dot in the Kondo regime, using the Kotliar-Ruckenstein slave-boson mean-field approach to the N-level Anderson model to define effectively noninteracting quasiparticles of the SU(N) Kondo system for N = 2 through 6, with Kondo-resonance transmission coefficients setting the linear noise from quasiparticle partitioning.

To treat nonlinear conductance, susceptibilities, and shot noise in the strong-coupling regime, they apply Fermi-liquid theory with parameters expressed through pseudofermion susceptibilities obtained within the slave-boson scheme, finding nonlinear shot noise dominated by two-quasiparticle scattering.
They show that away from the electron-hole symmetry point, three-body correlations must be included to capture the noise correctly.

summary_segments.note: Shot noise as a Kondo probe
summary_segments.text: Shot noise and its Fano factor reveal the effective charge and statistics of current-carrying quasiparticles; in SU(N) Kondo systems the backscattering quasiparticles carry fractionalized effective charge, and Fermi-liquid corrections including three-body terms are central to the modern theory of nonequilibrium Kondo noise.

significance_segments.text: Quantifying how two- and three-body correlations shape SU(N) Kondo shot noise away from particle-hole symmetry refines the predicted noise signatures used to identify the symmetry and quasiparticle charge of Kondo states in multi-level quantum dots.

title: Floquet analysis of coherence in periodically driven diamond NV ensemble systems
abstract: High-density nitrogen-vacancy (NV) ensembles are promising platforms for solid-state quantum sensing, but their performance is limited by dipolar interactions and inhomogeneous dephasing. Periodic decoupling sequences such as Waugh-Huber-Haeberlen (WAHUHA) can extend the observed stroboscopic decay time. However, it remains unclear that a longer effective dephasing time yield improved magnetic-field sensitivity. Here, we show that WAHUHA control increases the effective inhomogeneous dephasing time of a dense NV ensemble from $T_2^\ast$ of 0.9 $μ$s to $T_{2,eff}^\ast$ of 31 $μ$s, while producing little improvement in dc magnetic-field sensitivity. Using detuning-resolved stroboscopic spectroscopy and finite-pulse Floquet analysis, we show that the long-lived signal arises from phase wrapping and quasi-energy branch folding of the one-cycle unitary. These effects reshape the stroboscopic spectrum and suppress the detuning-to-phase transduction slope, $dΦ/dΔ$, which governs the dc magnetic-field response. Our results demonstrate that, under periodic driving, an extended effective dephasing time does not necessarily translate into enhanced dc sensitivity and establish finite-pulse Floquet analysis as a practical framework for evaluating coherence in spin ensembles.

summary_segments.text: The authors analyze coherence in periodically driven dense nitrogen-vacancy ensembles, showing that WAHUHA dynamical-decoupling control increases the effective inhomogeneous dephasing time from T2* of 0.9 microseconds to 31 microseconds while producing little improvement in dc magnetic-field sensitivity.

Using detuning-resolved stroboscopic spectroscopy and finite-pulse Floquet analysis, they trace the long-lived signal to phase wrapping and quasi-energy branch folding of the one-cycle unitary, which reshape the stroboscopic spectrum and suppress the detuning-to-phase transduction slope dPhi/dDelta that governs the dc response.

The results show that under periodic driving an extended effective dephasing time does not necessarily improve dc sensitivity, and establish finite-pulse Floquet analysis as a practical framework for evaluating coherence in spin ensembles.

Dynamical decoupling extends coherence by averaging out dipolar interactions and inhomogeneous broadening, but it also filters the signal of interest; for dc magnetometry, longer T2* is only useful if the field-to-phase transduction survives, a trade-off central to optimizing NV-ensemble magnetometers.

significance_segments.text: By disentangling effective coherence time from usable sensitivity under periodic driving, the work corrects a common assumption in NV-ensemble sensing and offers a Floquet-based criterion for when decoupling sequences genuinely improve magnetometric performance.

title: Ultra-high Q-factor superconducting tantalum resonators on 300 mm Si wafers
abstract: Superconducting resonators are central to superconducting quantum information technologies and essential for bosonic qubit architectures, where long-lived storage modes enable hardware-efficient error correction. Achieving ultra-high quality factors in scalable planar circuits is challenging because multiple dissipation channels contribute to the total loss. Here we report planar $α$-Ta resonators fabricated on 300 mm ultra-high-resistivity ($>10$ k$Ω$ cm) intrinsic silicon using industrial processes, achieving median internal Q factors exceeding 40 million and maxima above 60 million. Energy-participation-ratio analysis identifies a dominant participation-controlled interface loss mechanism and places conservative upper bounds on substrate-associated dissipation. For the best-performing substrate, the inferred substrate loss tangent is below $1.0 \times 10^{-8}$, establishing industrial MCZ silicon among the lowest-loss substrate platforms reported for superconducting resonators. At the same time, the exceptionally low losses show no clear correlation with commonly cited silicon substrate metrics such as room-temperature resistivity or impurity concentrations. More broadly, these studies establish industrial 300 mm processing, careful interface engineering, and 300 mm MCZ silicon substrates as a promising platform for resonator-heavy superconducting quantum architectures with ultra-high quality factors.

summary_segments.text: The authors report planar alpha-Ta superconducting resonators fabricated on 300 mm ultra-high-resistivity (>10 kOhm cm) intrinsic silicon using industrial processes, achieving median internal quality factors above 40 million with maxima exceeding 60 million.

Energy-participation-ratio analysis identifies a dominant participation-controlled interface loss mechanism and bounds substrate-associated dissipation, with the best substrate showing an inferred loss tangent below 1.0e-8, among the lowest reported; notably the losses show no clear correlation with common silicon metrics such as room-temperature resistivity or impurity concentration.
The results establish industrial 300 mm processing, careful interface engineering, and MCZ silicon substrates as a promising platform for resonator-heavy superconducting quantum architectures.

summary_segments.note: Why ultra-high Q on 300 mm matters
summary_segments.text: Bosonic-qubit and error-correction architectures rely on long-lived storage resonators, and tantalum has emerged as a leading low-loss superconductor; demonstrating these quality factors using foundry-compatible 300 mm wafers, rather than lab-scale processes, is central to scaling such hardware.

significance_segments.text: Reaching internal Q above 10^7 with industrial 300 mm fabrication links ultra-high-coherence superconducting resonators to scalable manufacturing, and the absence of correlation with standard substrate metrics points to interface engineering rather than bulk silicon selection as the key lever for further improvement.

title: On pseudogap phase as precursor to a superconducting dome in high-Tc cuprates: Non-analytic T* as a function of doping
abstract: We generalize the condition under which a quantum material exhibiting a pseudogap phase is a precursor to a superconducting (SC) dome. The result reveals the non-analytic T* as a function of doping. A well-known example is the high-Tc cuprates. Essentially, the SC dome is generated under two conditions: (1) that the pseudogap T* is a decreasing function of doping, due to the decrease in size of extended pairing of doped holes with doping, and most importantly, (2) that the rate of configurational-ordering parameter is an increasing function of doping as a result of the decrease in extended length of the disordered pairs. These two conditions are provided by the new entanglement and confinement pairing mechanism of high-Tc cuprates. This is a theory that has recently been discussed in the literature by Buot et al. It hinges on a novel strong entanglement and confinement hole pairing (ECHP) mechanism that unravels the microscopic features of the entire phase diagram of both electron and hole-doped high-Tc cuprates.

summary_segments.text: The author generalizes the condition under which a pseudogap phase acts as precursor to a superconducting dome, finding a non-analytic dependence of the pseudogap temperature T* on doping, with high-Tc cuprates as the canonical example.

The dome is argued to require two conditions: that T* decreases with doping due to shrinking extended pairing of doped holes, and that the rate of configurational ordering increases with doping as the disordered-pair extent decreases, both supplied by an entanglement-and-confinement hole-pairing mechanism for the cuprates.
The framework is presented as part of a theory previously discussed in the literature aiming to capture the full phase diagram of both electron- and hole-doped cuprates.

Whether the pseudogap is a precursor to superconductivity (preformed pairs) or a competing order remains a central unresolved question in cuprate physics; the doping dependence and end-point of T* relative to the superconducting dome are key empirical constraints on competing scenarios.

significance_segments.text: Predicting a non-analytic T*(doping) tied to a specific pairing mechanism offers a falsifiable structural feature that can be compared against the measured pseudogap line in cuprates, bearing on the long-debated relationship between the pseudogap and the superconducting dome.

title: Magnetic HIP-NN for spin dynamics in disordered itinerant magnets
abstract: We present a magnetic extension of the Hierarchically Interacting Particle Neural Network (HIP-NN) that enables large-scale simulations of electron-mediated spin dynamics in disordered itinerant magnets. The resulting magnetic HIP-NN (mHIP-NN) incorporates rotationally invariant spin correlations directly into hierarchical message-passing layers, enabling the network to learn emergent magnetic energy landscapes and effective local fields from coupled geometric-spin environments while preserving spin-rotation symmetry. As a benchmark application, we consider structurally disordered itinerant $s$-$d$ exchange models in which the effective magnetic forces arise dynamically from the instantaneous electronic structure and are computationally prohibitive to evaluate using conventional exact-diagonalization-based approaches. We show that mHIP-NN accurately reproduces the local torques governing Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert dynamics and faithfully captures the nonequilibrium evolution of spatial spin correlations following thermal quenches. Our results establish symmetry-aware hierarchical message-passing networks as an efficient and scalable framework for large-scale simulations of frustrated itinerant spin systems and nonequilibrium magnetic dynamics. More broadly, because the learned energy functional remains fully differentiable with respect to both atomic coordinates and spin variables, the framework also provides a natural foundation for spin-dependent interatomic potentials and coupled atom-spin dynamics.

summary_segments.text: The authors present a magnetic extension of the Hierarchically Interacting Particle Neural Network (mHIP-NN) for large-scale simulation of electron-mediated spin dynamics in disordered itinerant magnets, incorporating rotationally invariant spin correlations into hierarchical message-passing layers so the network learns emergent magnetic energy landscapes and effective local fields while preserving spin-rotation symmetry.

Benchmarked on structurally disordered itinerant s-d exchange models where magnetic forces arise from the instantaneous electronic structure and are prohibitive for exact-diagonalization methods, mHIP-NN accurately reproduces the local torques governing Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert dynamics and captures the nonequilibrium evolution of spatial spin correlations after thermal quenches.

Because the learned energy functional is differentiable with respect to both atomic coordinates and spin variables, the framework also provides a foundation for spin-dependent interatomic potentials and coupled atom-spin dynamics.

Electron-mediated (RKKY-type) interactions in itinerant magnets require recomputing the electronic structure at each spin configuration, which is the bottleneck for large-scale spin dynamics; symmetry-aware machine-learned force fields are an increasingly standard route to bypass this cost while retaining ab initio accuracy.

significance_segments.text: A symmetry-preserving, differentiable surrogate for electron-mediated spin forces makes large-scale, long-time simulations of frustrated itinerant magnets and nonequilibrium magnetic dynamics tractable, extending the reach of spin-dynamics studies well beyond exact diagonalization.

title: Inherent flux crosstalk and coupler-driven single-qubit gates in superconducting circuits
abstract: Crosstalk refers to unwanted qubit addressing. This is particularly detrimental when scaling up quantum information systems because unintended interactions limit their overall performance. For superconducting qubits, tunable couplings and frequency tunability achieved through externally applied magnetic fluxes enable high-fidelity entangling gates; however, they also introduce crosstalk through unintended flux coupling. In this work, we investigate the impact of time-dependent external magnetic fluxes in quantized circuits on superconducting qubit couplings. We find that non-trivial cross-voltage driving emerges between capacitively linked qubits when the magnetic flux threading the SQUID loop of a qubit varies in time, in a manner analogous to Faraday's law of induction. Crucially, we show that this effect enables fast single qubit control through the coupler element in standard tunable-coupler architectures, potentially eliminating the need for individual microwave $XY$ control lines.

summary_segments.text: The authors investigate how time-dependent external magnetic fluxes in quantized superconducting circuits affect qubit couplings, the same fluxes that enable tunable couplings and high-fidelity entangling gates but also introduce crosstalk through unintended flux coupling.
They find that a nontrivial cross-voltage drive emerges between capacitively linked qubits when the flux threading a qubit's SQUID loop varies in time, analogous to Faraday's law of induction.

Crucially, this effect can be turned into a resource: it enables fast single-qubit control through the coupler element in standard tunable-coupler architectures, potentially eliminating the need for individual microwave XY control lines.

Flux crosstalk is normally treated purely as an error channel that limits scaling of tunable-coupler processors; reinterpreting an inductively induced cross-drive as a control mechanism follows a recurring theme of converting parasitic couplings into usable gates and reducing control-line overhead.

significance_segments.text: Showing that coupler flux can drive single-qubit rotations suggests a resource-efficient control scheme that lowers wiring and microwave-line overhead in tunable-coupler architectures, while also clarifying the microscopic origin of an important crosstalk channel.

title: Interplay between Aharonov-Bohm and Altshuler-Aronov-Spivak oscillations in phase-pure GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires of different lengths
abstract: In GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires, comprising a tubular conducting shell, interference phenomena observed under an axial field and originating from closed-loop states encircling the insulating core, provide an ideal platform for superconducting quantum devices that utilize effects such as Aharonov--Bohm or Altshuler--Aronov--Spivak-type conductance oscillations. Both effects are different in nature with respect to phase rigidity because of interference of non-time-reversed or time-reversed paths, respectively. Since their occurrence is largely governed by averaging effects, which depend on sample dimensions and the transport regime, we present a systematic study of flux-periodic oscillations of phase-pure zinc-blende GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires as a function of gate voltage for samples with different contact separation lengths. Our analysis shows that with increasing contact separation length, averaging effects result in gradual reduction of $h/e$-periodic Aharonov--Bohm-type oscillations, while the $h/2e$-periodic Altshuler--Aronov--Spivak oscillations and its $h/4e$-periodic higher harmonics are enhanced. The additional phase rigidity seen in the $h/3e$-periodic oscillations is attributed to phase rigidity propagating from the neighbouring lower harmonics. Our tight-binding transport simulations on nanowires of different lengths which contain only a few scattering centers confirm the experimental observations regarding the different harmonics and their phase rigidity. Together, our experimental and simulation findings indicate quasi-ballistic transport with persistent Aharonov--Bohm-, and phase-rigid Altshuler--Aronov--Spivak-type oscillations despite few scattering centers.

summary_segments.text: In tubular GaAs/InAs core/shell nanowires, the authors systematically study flux-periodic conductance oscillations under an axial field as a function of gate voltage for phase-pure zinc-blende samples with different contact-separation lengths, distinguishing Aharonov-Bohm oscillations (non-time-reversed single-loop paths) from Altshuler-Aronov-Spivak oscillations (time-reversed paths).

They find that with increasing contact separation, averaging gradually suppresses the h/e-periodic Aharonov-Bohm oscillations while the h/2e Altshuler-Aronov-Spivak oscillations and their h/4e higher harmonics are enhanced, and they attribute the phase rigidity of the h/3e component to rigidity propagating from neighboring lower harmonics.

Tight-binding transport simulations on nanowires of different lengths with only a few scattering centers reproduce the observed harmonics and their phase rigidity, indicating quasi-ballistic transport with persistent AB and phase-rigid AAS oscillations.

Closed-loop states encircling an insulating core make core/shell nanowires a testbed for phase-coherent transport and a building block for proposed superconducting and topological devices; the balance between phase-rigid AAS and the more fragile AB component diagnoses the coherence length and disorder averaging in the shell.

significance_segments.text: Mapping how device length tunes the relative weight and phase rigidity of AB versus AAS harmonics gives a quantitative handle on phase coherence in tubular nanowire conductors, informing the design of hybrid devices that rely on flux-periodic coherence around the core.

title: Chiral anomaly and planar Hall conductance in pseudospin-$1$ Fermions
abstract: Positive longitudinal magnetoconductance (LMC) and planar Hall conductance (PHC) are hallmark transport signatures of the chiral anomaly in Weyl semimetals. Recent theoretical developments have extended Weyl fermions to multifold Fermionic systems with higher-pseudospin quasiparticle excitations, motivating the study of their magnetotransport properties. Here, we employ semiclassical Boltzmann transport theory within the relaxation-time approximation to investigate magnetotransport in pseudospin-1 Weyl semimetals, incorporating momentum-dependent scattering, orbital magnetic moment corrections, and charge-conservation constraints. To obtain a finite PHC, we break azimuthal symmetry through either a generic tilt of the quasiparticle dispersion or a finite misalignment between the electric and magnetic fields. In the untilted case, the PHC is positive and scales quadratically with magnetic field strength. Increasing the scattering strength induces a sign reversal of the PHC, producing a transition from positive to negative values. The PHC further exhibits the characteristic angular dependence $\sin 2γ$, where $γ$ is the angle between the magnetic field and the $x$-axis. Tilt qualitatively alters this behavior: tilt along the $x$- and $z$-directions changes the angular response to $\sinγ$ and $\cosγ$, respectively, generating strong anisotropy in the planar Hall signal. Moreover, the PHC shows a nonmonotonic dependence on tilt magnitude, revealing the interplay between tilt-induced symmetry breaking and chiral-anomaly-driven transport. Our results provide experimentally accessible signatures of multifold fermions and a framework for interpreting magnetotransport measurements in candidate materials of space groups 199, 214, and 220.

summary_segments.text: Using semiclassical Boltzmann transport in the relaxation-time approximation, the author computes the longitudinal magnetoconductance and planar Hall conductance of pseudospin-1 Weyl semimetals, incorporating momentum-dependent scattering, orbital-magnetic-moment corrections, and charge-conservation constraints; a finite planar Hall conductance requires breaking azimuthal symmetry via a dispersion tilt or a field misalignment.

In the untilted case the planar Hall conductance is positive and quadratic in field with the characteristic sin(2*gamma) angular dependence, but increasing scattering strength induces a sign reversal from positive to negative; tilt along x or z changes the angular response to sin(gamma) or cos(gamma) respectively, generating strong anisotropy.
