The planar Hall conductance also depends non-monotonically on tilt magnitude, and the results provide experimentally accessible signatures of multifold fermions in candidate materials of space groups 199, 214, and 220.

summary_segments.note: Multifold fermions and the chiral anomaly
summary_segments.text: Positive longitudinal magnetoconductance and a sin(2*gamma) planar Hall response are standard chiral-anomaly fingerprints in spin-1/2 Weyl semimetals; pseudospin-1 (multifold) fermions carry enhanced Berry curvature and chirality, so quantitative differences in their magnetotransport help distinguish higher-pseudospin band crossings in real materials.

significance_segments.text: Predicting tilt- and scattering-dependent sign changes and angular signatures of the planar Hall conductance gives concrete criteria to identify chiral-anomaly transport from multifold fermions, guiding magnetotransport experiments on candidate multifold semimetals.

title: Analog Quantum Asynchronous Event-Based Graph Neural Network
abstract: Asynchronous, event-based graph neural networks (AEGNNs) have recently emerged as an efficient paradigm for processing the sparse and high-temporal-resolution data from event cameras. In this paper, we propose quantum analog AEGNNs (QA-AEGNNs), a novel framework to implement an AEGNN on a neutral-atom quantum computer. Neutral-atom quantum processors offer a programmable analog quantum computing platform based on controllable Rydberg-atom interactions. To this end, we map the streaming event data to an array of trapped neutral atoms, where each atom represents a graph node (event) and is positioned such that geometric proximity reflects the spatio-temporal neighborhood of events. The native Rydberg Hamiltonian of the quantum processor is programmed to mirror the message-passing computations of the AEGNN, with atomic qubit states serving as node feature embeddings and inter-atom interactions realizing graph edges. Furthermore, we propose a hybrid quantum-classical training scheme in which the analog Hamiltonian parameters (e.g., laser pulse amplitudes and detunings) are optimized using classical feedback to learn the quantum AEGNN model from data. Our approach leverages the continuous Hamiltonian dynamics and massive parallelism of neutral-atom quantum systems to natively execute event-based graph computations with potential accuracy improvements

summary_segments.text: The authors propose quantum analog asynchronous event-based graph neural networks (QA-AEGNNs) implemented on a neutral-atom quantum computer, mapping streaming event-camera data to an array of trapped atoms where each atom is a graph node positioned so geometric proximity reflects the spatio-temporal neighborhood of events.
The native Rydberg Hamiltonian is programmed to mirror the message-passing computation, with atomic qubit states serving as node feature embeddings and inter-atom interactions realizing graph edges.

They propose a hybrid quantum-classical training scheme in which analog Hamiltonian parameters such as laser amplitudes and detunings are optimized via classical feedback, leveraging the continuous Hamiltonian dynamics and parallelism of neutral-atom systems to natively execute event-based graph computations.

Neutral-atom processors naturally encode connectivity through atom placement and Rydberg blockade, making them well suited to graph-structured problems; recasting an event-based GNN as analog Rydberg dynamics fits the broader effort to find native machine-learning workloads for analog quantum hardware.

significance_segments.text: Mapping a sparse, event-driven graph neural network onto programmable Rydberg dynamics is a concrete proposal for analog quantum machine learning, identifying the hardware requirements and a potential advantage in communication complexity for processing event-camera data.

title: Quantum statistics in an extended collider coupled to a qubit
abstract: Mesoscopic colliders provide an effective platform for probing the mutual statistics of quantum particles. Recent experiments have successfully extracted the mutual statistics of fermions, and more exotic anyons using quantum point contacts (QPCs). Coupling a point-like collider, such as a quantum point contact, to a two-level impurity or qubit can induce statistical transmutation of fermions, causing them to display boson-like bunching tendencies. Here, we extend the analysis to an extended collider. We investigate the scattering of two incoming fermionic and bosonic wave packets in the presence of post-selection on the impurity state, and systematically analyze the possible benchmarks used to characterize bunching and infer the underlying mutual statistics. We show that only a specific benchmark faithfully captures the mutual statistics of the colliding particles, while alternative choices can produce spurious statistical signatures. Hence, the correct benchmark for probing the quantum statistics depends on the intricate details of the mesoscopic collider.

summary_segments.text: Building on the fact that coupling a point-like collider such as a quantum point contact to a two-level impurity can statistically transmute fermions into boson-like bunching behavior, the authors extend the analysis to an extended collider and study the scattering of two incoming fermionic and bosonic wave packets with post-selection on the impurity state.

They systematically analyze the benchmarks used to characterize bunching and infer mutual statistics, showing that only a specific benchmark faithfully captures the mutual statistics of the colliding particles while alternative choices can produce spurious statistical signatures.
The correct benchmark for probing quantum statistics therefore depends on the detailed structure of the mesoscopic collider.

Hong-Ou-Mandel-type mesoscopic colliders at quantum point contacts have recently been used to measure the exchange statistics of electrons and fractional anyons; understanding how qubit coupling and collider geometry bias the inferred statistics is important for interpreting such cross-correlation noise experiments.

significance_segments.text: Identifying which observables genuinely reflect mutual statistics, versus those that yield spurious signatures, provides a needed methodological check for collider-based measurements of anyonic and statistically transmuted particles in mesoscopic devices.

title: Magnetism and Topology from Circularly Polarized Phonon Floquet Engineering
abstract: We theoretically show that circularly polarized phonons induce electronic magnetization and drive a topological phase transition via phonon Floquet engineering. Considering the electronic states modulated by circularly polarized phonons on a honeycomb lattice, we show that such lattice dynamics generates an effective next-nearest-neighbor electron hopping, leading to a Haldane-type mass term. Circularly polarized phonon breaks time-reversal symmetry (TRS) and opens a gap at valley points, undergoing phase transition from a trivial insulator to a Chern insulator. Moreover, the orbital and spin magnetizations emerge due to the breaking of TRS. Our results show that circularly polarized phonons serve as an effective magnetic field to engineer magnetism and topology, offering new opportunities for phonon Floquet approaches.

summary_segments.text: The authors show theoretically that circularly polarized phonons induce electronic magnetization and drive a topological phase transition via phonon Floquet engineering: on a honeycomb lattice, the phonon-modulated electronic states generate an effective next-nearest-neighbor hopping that produces a Haldane-type mass term.

The circularly polarized phonon breaks time-reversal symmetry and opens a gap at the valley points, driving a transition from a trivial insulator to a Chern insulator, while orbital and spin magnetizations emerge from the broken time-reversal symmetry.
The phonons thus act as an effective magnetic field that engineers magnetism and topology, offering new opportunities for phonon-based Floquet approaches.

Floquet engineering of topology has largely used circularly polarized light to realize Haldane-like masses; using chiral phonons instead leverages a lattice degree of freedom that can carry angular momentum, potentially achieving time-reversal breaking and topological gaps without external optical driving or static magnetic fields.

significance_segments.text: Proposing chiral phonons as an effective magnetic field for inducing magnetization and Chern-insulator topology broadens Floquet engineering to lattice dynamics, suggesting a route to dynamically control magnetic and topological properties without external fields at equilibrium.

title: Relativistic Effects in Spin Correlations Induced by QED Scattering and Wigner Rotations
abstract: We study the relativistic nature of the interactions that, at tree level, generate spin correlations between two electrons in Møller scattering, as well as in an extended process involving a witness particle $C$. The corresponding processes, $e^{-}e^{-}\rightarrow e^{-}e^{-}$ and $e^{-}e^{-}C\rightarrow e^{-}e^{-}C$, are analyzed both in the center-of-mass frame and, for the former process, in a Lorentz-boosted frame where Wigner rotations arise. It is found that, through a nonrelativistic approximation of the scattering amplitudes, dipole-dipole and current-dipole interactions are responsible for the emergence of these correlations. This is evidenced by the variation of the von Neumann entropy of one electron for initially separable states, and of $C$ for an initially prepared three-particle entangled W-state. In Wigner rotations, the invariance of entropy under local unitary transformations is maintained at the expense of the emergence of quantum coherence in the density matrix at large rapidities. As a consequence, the final states of both particles are evaluated and shown to encode information about the scattering process through their spin expectation values. This framework is then used to comment on the correlations in the inelastic process $e^{-}e^{+}\rightarrowμ^{-}μ^{+}$, for which some research has reported differing results.

summary_segments.text: The authors study how tree-level QED interactions generate spin correlations between two electrons in Moller scattering and in an extended process with a witness particle C, analyzing e-e -> e-e and e-e-C -> e-e-C in the center-of-mass frame and, for the former, in a Lorentz-boosted frame where Wigner rotations arise.

Through a nonrelativistic approximation of the scattering amplitudes they identify dipole-dipole and current-dipole interactions as responsible for the correlations, evidenced by changes in the von Neumann entropy of one electron for initially separable states and of C for an initially prepared three-particle W-state.

Under Wigner rotations the invariance of entropy under local unitaries is preserved at the expense of emergent quantum coherence in the density matrix at large rapidities, and the framework is used to comment on the inelastic process e-e+ -> mu-mu+ where prior results have differed.

Computing entanglement and spin correlations generated by elementary QED scattering has become an active topic linking quantum information to high-energy physics, with Wigner rotations of spin under Lorentz boosts being the well-known subtlety that makes relativistic entanglement frame-dependent at the level of reduced states.

significance_segments.text: By tracing scattering-generated spin correlations to specific interaction terms and tracking how Wigner rotations redistribute entropy and coherence across frames, the work informs the interpretation of entanglement and Bell-inequality tests in high-energy collision processes.

title: Fabry-Perot Interference, g-factor Anisotropy, and Gate-Tunable Quantum dot in Chiral Tellurium Nanowires
abstract: Chiral materials with strong spin-orbit coupling offer a unique platform for exploring the interplay between topology, chirality, and quantum transport yet the quantum coherent regime in elemental tellurium nanostructures remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate phase-coherent quasi-ballistic transport, anisotropic Zeeman spectroscopy, and gate-tunable quantum dot formation in hydrothermally grown t-tellurium nanowires. Single nanowire field-effect transistors exhibit p-type transport with hole mobilities rising from approx. 80 cm2 V-1 s-1 at 210 K to approx. 190 cm2 V-1 s-1 at 1 K, consistent with a crossover from phonon-limited to Coulomb scattering dominated regimes near 50 K. Notably, devices segregate into two distinct regimes based on their room temperature two-terminal resistance : low-resistance devices (< 30 kOhm) exhibit Fabry-Perot interference, whereas high resistance devices (> 30 kOhm) display Coulomb-blockade behavior revealing a two-terminal resistance-driven transition between quasi-ballistic and strongly localized transport regimes. Zeeman spectroscopy in in-plane and out-of-plane magnetic fields yields highly anisotropic Lande g-factors (an in-plane gparallel = 1.18 and an out-of-plane gperp = 18.41) and directly resolves a spin-orbit energy gap DeltaSO = 0.864 meV from an avoided crossing. These results establish chiral tellurium nanowires as a versatile platform for gate-defined spin qubits exploiting large, tunable g-factors and for hybrid tellurium-superconductor architectures targeting Majorana zero modes in an elemental vdW system.

summary_segments.text: The authors demonstrate phase-coherent quasi-ballistic transport, anisotropic Zeeman spectroscopy, and gate-tunable quantum dot formation in hydrothermally grown chiral (trigonal) tellurium nanowires, with single-nanowire FETs showing p-type transport and hole mobilities rising from about 80 to 190 cm2/Vs between 210 K and 1 K, consistent with a crossover from phonon-limited to Coulomb-scattering-dominated regimes near 50 K.

Devices segregate by room-temperature two-terminal resistance: low-resistance devices (<30 kOhm) show Fabry-Perot interference while high-resistance devices (>30 kOhm) show Coulomb blockade, revealing a resistance-driven transition between quasi-ballistic and strongly localized transport.

Zeeman spectroscopy yields highly anisotropic Lande g-factors (in-plane 1.18, out-of-plane 18.41) and resolves a spin-orbit gap of 0.864 meV from an avoided crossing, establishing chiral tellurium nanowires as a platform for gate-defined spin qubits and hybrid tellurium-superconductor architectures targeting Majorana zero modes.

summary_segments.note: Why elemental Te is of interest
summary_segments.text: Tellurium is a chiral elemental semiconductor with strong spin-orbit coupling and a radial spin texture; its large, anisotropic g-factors and resolvable spin-orbit gap make it attractive for spin-orbit qubits and for proximity-induced topological superconductivity in a single-element van der Waals material.

significance_segments.text: Demonstrating the quantum-coherent regime, tunable quantum dots, and a directly measured spin-orbit gap in elemental tellurium nanowires positions this previously underexplored material as a candidate platform for spin-orbit qubits and hybrid superconducting devices.

title: The dynamic 4.8.8 Floquet code
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum memories depend on the syndrome extraction circuit as much as on the underlying code. Ancilla-free or dynamic circuits are an effective way to improve this circuit layer. For the 6.6.6 honeycomb Floquet code, making the circuit dynamic raises the threshold and lowers the qubit overhead, but at the cost of halving the spatial code distance. A dynamic construction for the 4.8.8 lattice layout was conjectured to preserve full distance. I confirm this and give a dynamic measurement circuit for the CSS 4.8.8 Floquet code. To benchmark it, I construct and compare four circuit-level implementations on a torus, including two dynamic variants (with and without mid-circuit resets), the standard ancilla-based circuit, and a pipelined ancilla-based circuit. Under circuit-level depolarising noise, the reset dynamic circuit reaches a per-round threshold of $0.463\%$ $(0.490\%)$ with MWPM (BP+matching), while the no-reset variant reaches the highest threshold of all four circuits at $0.512\%$ $(0.574\%)$. The standard ancilla-based circuit only achieves $0.228\%$ $(0.240\%)$, but the pipelined schedule reaches $0.478\%$ $(0.489\%)$. The reset dynamic circuit also has a faster-growing timelike distance, with $2\le d_t/n_{\mathrm{qec}}\le 3$ asymptotically against a tight $3/2$ for the other three, and running it for fewer rounds gives the smallest spacetime volume in the fast-reset regime, while the no-reset variant is smallest in the slow-reset regime. The 4.8.8 dynamic circuits therefore see the expected threshold gain and overhead reduction without the spatial-distance cost, demonstrating the advantage of dynamic syndrome extraction in Floquet codes.

summary_segments.text: The author confirms a conjectured dynamic (ancilla-free) measurement circuit for the CSS 4.8.8 Floquet code that, unlike the dynamic 6.6.6 honeycomb code, preserves the full spatial code distance rather than halving it.

Benchmarking four circuit-level implementations on a torus under depolarizing noise, the reset dynamic circuit reaches a per-round threshold of 0.463% (0.490% with BP+matching) and the no-reset variant the highest at 0.512% (0.574%), versus 0.228% for the standard ancilla-based circuit and 0.478% for a pipelined ancilla-based schedule.

The reset dynamic circuit also has a faster-growing timelike distance and the smallest spacetime volume in the fast-reset regime, so the 4.8.8 dynamic circuits realize the expected threshold gain and overhead reduction without the spatial-distance cost incurred by the 6.6.6 case.

Floquet codes extract stabilizer information through sequences of two-qubit measurements; making the syndrome circuit dynamic (ancilla-free) can raise thresholds and cut overhead but sometimes sacrifices code distance, so a construction that keeps full distance is directly relevant to practical fault-tolerance overheads.

significance_segments.text: Showing that dynamic syndrome extraction can improve threshold and overhead for the 4.8.8 Floquet code without distance reduction strengthens the case for ancilla-free circuits in Floquet-code memories and gives concrete circuit-level numbers for architecture trade-offs.

title: Radiowave-induced Resistance Oscillations
abstract: Microwave-induced resistance oscillations (MIROs) \cite{zudov:2001a} occur when a 2D electron gas is subjected to radiation of frequency $ω= 2 πf$ and varying magnetic field $B$. MIROs are periodic in $1/B$, with the period determined by the radiation frequency $ω$, and their amplitude scales with the radiation power. Stepping from single-photon transitions between Landau levels, MIROs are found on the lower-field side of the cyclotron resonance, $ω_c \lesssim ω$, where $ω_c$ is the cyclotron frequency. Here, we report on experimental observation of another class of magneto resistance oscillations, which are also induced by radiation, but in the radio frequency (UHF band) range. These oscillations are distinct from MIROs in the following aspects: (i) they occur at $ω_c \gg ω$, (ii) their amplitude is independent of radiation power, (iii) their period is controlled by the radiation electric field, rather than by $ω$, and (iv) they can be either $1/B$ or $1/B^2$-periodic, depending on $B$. We further show that these oscillations can be explained by a displacement model in the limit of short-range disorder.

summary_segments.text: The authors report a new class of radiation-induced magnetoresistance oscillations in high-mobility 2D electron gases driven in the radio-frequency (UHF) range, distinct from the established microwave-induced resistance oscillations (MIROs).

Unlike MIROs, these radiowave-induced oscillations occur where the cyclotron frequency greatly exceeds the drive frequency, have an amplitude independent of radiation power, have a period set by the radiation electric field rather than the frequency, and can be either 1/B- or 1/B^2-periodic depending on B.
The authors show these oscillations can be explained by a displacement model in the limit of short-range disorder.

MIROs and the related zero-resistance states arise from photon-assisted Landau-level transitions and are central to nonequilibrium magnetotransport in 2DEGs; observing a power-independent, field-controlled oscillation at much lower frequencies points to a qualitatively different, nonlinear or multi-photon mechanism.

significance_segments.text: Extending radiation-induced magnetoresistance oscillations into the radio-frequency regime, with power-independent amplitude and field-set periodicity, reveals a transport phenomenon outside the standard single-photon MIRO picture and challenges theory to account for the displacement mechanism under short-range disorder.

title: Jittery Quantum Boomerang Effect
abstract: We study the dynamics of a spin-polarized wave packet in a disordered Rashba two-dimensional electron gas and identify a jittery quantum boomerang effect in which longitudinal and transverse motion return to the origin through fundamentally distinct mechanisms. Starting from an initial state with finite momentum along $x$ and spin polarized along $z$, we calculate the time evolution by combining a Chebyshev expansion of the time-evolution operator with a disorder ensemble average. In the weak-scattering regime, equations of motion derived from the quantum kinetic equation reproduce the numerical trends and show that impurity scattering acts as a viscous damping mechanism that suppresses the transient Zitterbewegung and drives the transverse displacement back to $y=0$ at long times. In contrast, the longitudinal dynamics show a Drude-like saturation at weak disorder. These results are consistent with the vanishing intrinsic spin Hall conductivity in the disordered Rashba model and with experimental observations of a transient intrinsic spin Hall effect in the time-domain. As disorder increases, the longitudinal dynamics evolve to a partial return toward the origin, which signals a transition from weak antilocalization to Anderson localization in 2D.
groups: Gerson J. Ferreira (Federal University of Uberlândia)

summary_segments.text: The authors study a spin-polarized wave packet in a disordered Rashba 2D electron gas and identify a jittery quantum boomerang effect in which longitudinal and transverse motion return to the origin through distinct mechanisms, starting from a state with finite momentum along x and spin along z, computed via a Chebyshev expansion of the time-evolution operator with disorder averaging.

In the weak-scattering regime, equations of motion from the quantum kinetic equation reproduce the numerics and show that impurity scattering acts as viscous damping that suppresses the transient Zitterbewegung and drives the transverse displacement back to zero, while the longitudinal dynamics show a Drude-like saturation.

These results are consistent with the vanishing intrinsic spin Hall conductivity of the disordered Rashba model and with observations of a transient intrinsic spin Hall effect; as disorder increases, the longitudinal dynamics evolve to a partial return, signaling a crossover from weak antilocalization to Anderson localization in 2D.

The quantum boomerang effect, where a wave packet with initial momentum returns to its origin, is a real-time signature of Anderson localization; adding Rashba spin-orbit coupling couples this to spin precession and Zitterbewegung, connecting localization dynamics to the much-debated fate of the intrinsic spin Hall effect under disorder.

significance_segments.text: Showing that longitudinal and transverse returns arise from genuinely different mechanisms, and linking the transverse return to the vanishing of the disorder-averaged spin Hall conductivity, ties real-time boomerang dynamics to the localization and spin-Hall physics of Rashba 2DEGs.

title: Planar Hall effect in single and bilayer Rashba systems
abstract: The planar Hall effect (PHE) is an anisotropic magnetotransport response generated by coplanar electric and magnetic fields. We investigate the PHE in single- and bilayer two-dimensional electron gases (2DEGs) with Rashba spin-orbit coupling and identify two distinct mechanisms: Zeeman coupling and a band geometric channel. In the Zeeman channel, an in-plane magnetic field distorts the Rashba spin-orbit-coupled band dispersion and generates anisotropic carrier velocities, producing a finite PHE. In an asymmetric Rashba bilayer, interlayer electronic delocalization generates finite planar Berry curvature and orbital magnetic moment components, giving rise to a band geometric PHE channel. Using semiclassical Boltzmann transport theory, we calculate the chemical potential and angular dependence of the planar Hall conductivity for both mechanisms. Symmetry analysis shows that the leading response is quadratic in the magnetic field and exhibits the characteristic $π$-periodic angular dependence. For the parameter regime considered here, the Zeeman-induced contribution dominates, while the band geometric channel provides a distinct symmetry-allowed contribution unique to asymmetric Rashba bilayers. Our results reveal microscopic origins of anisotropic magnetotransport in spin-orbit-coupled two-dimensional materials.

summary_segments.text: The authors investigate the planar Hall effect in single- and bilayer 2DEGs with Rashba spin-orbit coupling and identify two distinct mechanisms: a Zeeman channel, in which an in-plane field distorts the Rashba dispersion to generate anisotropic carrier velocities, and a band-geometric channel arising from planar Berry curvature and orbital magnetic moment in asymmetric Rashba bilayers with interlayer delocalization.

Using semiclassical Boltzmann theory, they compute the chemical-potential and angular dependence of the planar Hall conductivity for both mechanisms, with symmetry analysis showing the leading response is quadratic in field and pi-periodic in angle.
For the parameters considered the Zeeman contribution dominates, while the band-geometric channel provides a distinct symmetry-allowed contribution unique to asymmetric Rashba bilayers.

The planar Hall effect has become a sensitive probe of band geometry and spin texture in topological and spin-orbit-coupled materials; disentangling Zeeman-driven and Berry-curvature-driven channels in Rashba 2DEGs clarifies the microscopic origin of anisotropic magnetotransport in these widely studied systems.

significance_segments.text: Identifying a band-geometric planar Hall channel specific to asymmetric Rashba bilayers, distinguishable by its symmetry from the Zeeman channel, gives an experimental route to detect Berry-curvature contributions to magnetotransport in two-dimensional spin-orbit materials.

title: Frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy of a semiconductor charge qubit coupled to a high-impedance resonator
abstract: Superconducting resonators coupled to semiconductor quantum dots provide a powerful platform to investigate light-matter interaction and decoherence mechanisms in solid-state quantum systems. Here we study a hybrid circuit quantum electrodynamics architecture consisting of a GaAs double-quantum-dot charge qubit capacitively coupled to a high-impedance, frequency-tunable SQUID-array resonator. By tuning the qubit transition frequency over the range $ω_\mathrm{q}/2π\sim 3$-$6$ GHz, we perform frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy of the charge qubit across a broad energy window. Time-resolved measurements enable us to disentangle relaxation and pure dephasing processes and to identify distinct decoherence regimes as a function of qubit frequency. We find that at lower frequencies ($\leq 4.5$ GHz) dephasing dominates the qubit linewidth, whereas at higher frequencies energy relaxation becomes the leading contribution. The measured frequency dependence of the relaxation rate exhibits a cubic scaling, consistent with charge-qubit decay dominated by coupling to a piezoelectric phonon bath and providing frequency-resolved access to the corresponding phonon-induced spectral density. Our results show that hybrid semiconductor--superconducting circuits can serve as sensitive spectroscopic tools to probe microscopic decoherence mechanisms relevant for a wide range of hybrid quantum devices.
groups: Pasquale Scarlino (École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne)

summary_segments.text: The authors study a hybrid circuit-QED device consisting of a GaAs double-quantum-dot charge qubit capacitively coupled to a high-impedance, frequency-tunable SQUID-array resonator, and by tuning the qubit transition over roughly 3-6 GHz perform frequency-resolved decoherence spectroscopy across a broad energy window.

Time-resolved measurements disentangle relaxation from pure dephasing, showing that dephasing dominates the linewidth at lower frequencies (below about 4.5 GHz) while energy relaxation dominates at higher frequencies.

The relaxation rate exhibits cubic frequency scaling consistent with charge-qubit decay dominated by coupling to a piezoelectric phonon bath, giving frequency-resolved access to the phonon-induced spectral density and demonstrating that hybrid semiconductor-superconductor circuits can serve as sensitive probes of microscopic decoherence.

Charge noise, with its characteristic 1/f low-frequency component and phonon-dominated high-frequency tail, is the dominant decoherence source for GaAs charge qubits; using a tunable high-impedance resonator to spectrally resolve these contributions provides direct microscopic insight relevant across hybrid quantum-dot devices.

significance_segments.text: Frequency-resolved separation of dephasing and phonon-limited relaxation, including the cubic scaling diagnostic of piezoelectric phonons, turns a hybrid resonator-qubit device into a spectroscopic tool for the noise mechanisms that limit semiconductor and hybrid quantum systems.

title: Non-Bloch band theory of boundary-controlled magnon edge modes in an antiferromagnetic chain
abstract: We define a winding number within the Non-Bloch band theory framework that captures the emergence of magnon edge modes in a one-dimensional antiferromagnetic spin chain, even when the conventional Bloch winding number is trivial. Within linear spin-wave theory, magnon excitations are governed by a non-Hermitian dynamic matrix, despite the underlying Hamiltonian being Hermitian. The symmetry classification of this matrix yields a trivial bulk invariant, however, finite systems exhibit boundary-localized modes, signaling a breakdown of the conventional bulk-boundary correspondence. We further show that these edge modes can be controlled via boundary perturbations. By tuning the boundary potential, the modes can be driven into or out of the bulk spectrum. To resolve the bulk-boundary mismatch, we develop a non-Bloch framework based on a generalized Brillouin zone and a winding number that correctly predicts the presence of edge states. Our results establish boundary-controlled topological transitions that are experimentally accessible through local Zeeman fields or modified edge anisotropy in antiferromagnetic van der Waals nanostructures.

summary_segments.text: The authors define a winding number within Non-Bloch band theory that captures the emergence of magnon edge modes in a one-dimensional antiferromagnetic spin chain even when the conventional Bloch winding number is trivial, noting that within linear spin-wave theory magnon excitations are governed by a non-Hermitian dynamic matrix despite the underlying Hamiltonian being Hermitian.

The matrix's symmetry classification yields a trivial bulk invariant, yet finite systems exhibit boundary-localized modes, signaling a breakdown of conventional bulk-boundary correspondence; a non-Bloch framework based on a generalized Brillouin zone and an associated winding number correctly predicts these edge states.

The edge modes can be controlled by boundary perturbations, with the boundary potential tuning them into or out of the bulk spectrum, giving boundary-controlled topological transitions accessible via local Zeeman fields or modified edge anisotropy in antiferromagnetic van der Waals nanostructures.

Bosonic Bogoliubov-de Gennes problems generically yield non-Hermitian dynamic matrices, so non-Hermitian topological concepts such as the non-Bloch generalized Brillouin zone and the non-Hermitian skin effect apply to magnons; using them to predict and control magnon edge modes connects magnonics to the broader non-Hermitian topology program.

significance_segments.text: Providing a non-Bloch invariant that correctly predicts boundary-controlled magnon edge modes gives a design principle for magnonic boundary states tunable by local fields, relevant to engineering edge transport in antiferromagnetic van der Waals magnets.

title: Impact of gate-voltage noise on silicon spin-qubit variational quantum eigensolvers
abstract: Quantum computers offer a route to outperform classical methods in tasks such as molecular simulation, motivating hybrid algorithms like the Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) for near-term devices. Silicon spin qubits are a promising platform for scalable quantum computation, but their performance is limited by hardware imperfections -- most notably charge-noise-induced potential fluctuations and static miscalibration of gate-electrode voltages -- which degrade quantum gate fidelities and, ultimately, algorithmic accuracy. Here we develop a hardware-algorithm co-simulation framework for silicon quantum-dot processors that links 3D electrostatics to effective $g$-factors and exchange couplings, and propagates voltage-level noise through realistic control pulses. Using VQE for $\mathrm{H}_2$ ground-state energy estimation as a circuit-level testbed, we study both static scaling/offset errors on the gate-electrode voltages and stochastic fluctuations modeled as random-telegraph noise with tunable amplitudes and switching times. At the gate level, we show that exchange-based two-qubit gates are roughly an order of magnitude more sensitive to these types of noise than ESR-driven single-qubit rotations. Quantum process tomography and Kraus-operator analysis further distinguish coherent and incoherent contributions and quantify the fraction of error that is, in principle, correctable by a compensating unitary. Embedding these noise models into the VQE circuit, we identify regimes of miscalibration strength and noise switching time compatible with chemically accurate energy estimates, and discuss how statistical post-processing based on the full distribution of noisy energy estimates could further improve accuracy.

summary_segments.text: The authors develop a hardware-algorithm co-simulation framework for silicon quantum-dot processors that links 3D electrostatics to effective g-factors and exchange couplings and propagates voltage-level noise through realistic control pulses, using VQE for the H2 ground-state energy as a circuit-level testbed under both static scaling/offset gate-voltage errors and random-telegraph fluctuations.

At the gate level they find exchange-based two-qubit gates are roughly an order of magnitude more sensitive to gate-voltage noise than ESR-driven single-qubit rotations, and use quantum process tomography and Kraus-operator analysis to separate coherent from incoherent error and quantify the fraction correctable by a compensating unitary.

Embedding these noise models in the VQE circuit, they identify regimes of miscalibration strength and noise switching time compatible with chemically accurate energy estimates and discuss statistical post-processing of the noisy-energy distribution to further improve accuracy.

summary_segments.note: Charge noise in silicon spin qubits
summary_segments.text: Charge-noise-induced gate-voltage fluctuations, often modeled as random-telegraph or 1/f noise, are the leading limitation on silicon spin-qubit fidelity; connecting device electrostatics through gate pulses to algorithm-level VQE accuracy bridges hardware modeling and near-term quantum-chemistry benchmarks.

significance_segments.text: A device-to-algorithm co-simulation that pinpoints exchange gates as the dominant noise-sensitive element and maps noise parameters to chemically accurate VQE regimes gives concrete guidance for calibration tolerances and error mitigation in silicon spin-qubit processors.

title: Topological invariant responsible for the integer QHE and non-commutative geometry
abstract: We consider a wide class of $2D$ tight - binding models of solid state physics. These models are, in the most general case, non - homogeneous. The topological invariant ${\cal N}_3$ responsible for the quantization of the Hall conductivity, for the specific case of the integer quantum Hall effect in $2D$, is expressed through the Wigner transformation of the two-point electron Matsubara Green function. We express this invariant as a pairing of the element of the $K^{-1}$ group (generated by the Green function) with the specific element of the cyclic cohomology group $HC^3$. According to a set of local index theorems the values of ${\cal N}_3$ can be shown to be integer for a limited class of tight - binding models.

summary_segments.text: The authors consider a broad class of 2D tight-binding models, including non-homogeneous ones, and express the topological invariant N3 responsible for the quantization of the Hall conductivity in the integer quantum Hall effect through the Wigner transformation of the two-point electron Matsubara Green function.

They recast this invariant as a pairing between an element of the K^{-1} group generated by the Green function and a specific element of the cyclic cohomology group HC^3, and invoke local index theorems to argue N3 is integer-valued for a limited class of tight-binding models.

The integer quantum Hall conductivity has long been understood topologically, and the non-commutative-geometry approach (Bellissard and others) handles disordered, non-translation-invariant systems where Bloch theory fails; expressing the invariant via Wigner-transformed Green functions and cyclic cohomology continues this program toward inhomogeneous lattice models.

significance_segments.text: Casting the integer quantum Hall invariant in a Green-function, non-commutative-geometry language that applies to inhomogeneous and disordered tight-binding models offers a unified topological framework spanning clean and disordered systems, relevant to robustly characterizing Hall quantization beyond translationally invariant settings.
